Title: llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit_object() creates incorrect executable type on Windows · Issue #181 · numba/llvmlite · GitHub
URL: https://github.com/numba/llvmlite/issues/181
Content: ## Navigation Menu. # Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests... # Provide feedback. We read every piece of feedback, and take your input very seriously. # Saved searches. ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. To see all available qualifiers, see our documentation. # llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit\_object() creates incorrect executable type on Windows #181. ## Footer. I was messing around and trying to create an executable using llvmlite on my Windows machine, but I couldn't get it to work. I noticed that even though all of the default settings told me that the executable would be of the COFF format, it was an ELF executable. Here's an example shell run showing the issue:  . This generated file cannot be run by Windows. I'm running Windows 10, the beta that allows Bash on Windows, and Python 3.5 in a Conda Virtual Environment. I'm using the latest llvmlite version available on Conda. ### Type. ## Issue actions. ### Footer navigation.

Title: Building and using llvmlite - a basic example - Eli Bendersky's website
URL: https://eli.thegreenplace.net/2015/building-and-using-llvmlite-a-basic-example
Content: llvmlite was created last year by the developers of Numba (a JIT compiler for scientific Python), and just recently replaced llvmpy as their bridge to LLVM. One of the reasons the Numba devs decided to ditch llvmpy in favor of a new approach is the biggest issue heavy users of LLVM as a library have - its incredible rate of API-breaking change. The LLVM C++ API is notoriously unstable and will remain this way for the foreseeable future. Since building the IR using repeated FFI calls to LLVM proved to be slow and error-prone in llvmpy, llvmlite re-implemented the IR builder in pure Python. This also reduces the "API surface" llvmlite uses, since the textual representation of LLVM IR is one of the more stable things in LLVM. LLVM has a great tool named llvm-config in its binary image, and the Makefile in llvmlite uses it, which means building llvmlite with any version of LLVM I want is just a simple matter of running:.
